intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A natural-language interface to a relational database, intended to answer read-only analytical questions for internal data consumers via a configured SQLAlchemy connection. The agent may use sql_db_list_tables, sql_db_schema, sql_db_query_checker, and sql_db_query; output is a direct natural-language reply to the user and nothing else (no outbound HTTP, file writes, email, or caching). DML, DDL, administrative commands, and multi-statement queries are explicitly forbidden with no approval path — and the remit states that the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point for the DML/DDL prohibition.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python LangChain agent toolkit. create_sql_agent() in libs/community/langchain_community/agent_toolkits/sql/base.py constructs a legacy AgentExecutor wired to four tools defined in libs/community/langchain_community/tools/sql_database/tool.py (QuerySQLDatabaseTool, InfoSQLDatabaseTool, ListSQLDatabaseTool, QuerySQLCheckerTool); all four hold a reference to a shared SQLDatabase wrapper in libs/community/langchain_community/utilities/sql_database.py whose run_no_throw() hands the LLM-authored query string directly to SQLAlchemy via _execute(). There is no SQL parser, no statement-class allow-list, no DML/DDL rejector, no logging in the slice, and no enforced ordering between the checker tool and the executor tool. The system prompt (SQL_PREFIX in agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py) carries the only operative DML/DDL prohibition; the create_sql_agent docstring (lines 71-97 of base.py) explicitly tells operators that least-privilege roles, statement timeouts, and query guardrails must be applied server-side.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in prose, zero code-level enforcement: the remit's hard prohibitions on DML, DDL, administrative commands, and multi-statement queries are carried only by the SQL_PREFIX system-prompt string and the create_sql_agent docstring warning — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() passes the LLM-authored SQL straight to SQLDatabase.run_no_throw(), which calls SQLAlchemy's connection.execute() with no statement-class check, no parser, no allow-list. The remit itself anticipates this and states (R-20) that the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point; in this slice it is.

Compounding this, tool outputs — table names, column names, sample rows, and query results — flow back into LLM context unlabeled, so a row inserted into any reachable table is an indirect-injection path into the next query. With zero logging calls anywhere in the workspace, an injection-driven DML/DDL or admin-command run leaves no audit trail. Real but narrow operative controls are present (max_iterations=15 default, top_k threaded into the prompt, the sanitize_schema helper, and a clear operator-facing security docstring) but they do not address the central exec-of-LLM-output divergence.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Tools the agent may use
rules.rule_text: sql_db_list_tables, sql_db_schema, sql_db_query_checker, sql_db_query
The specific database the agent is configured to connect to, scoped to the tables exposed by the SQLDatabase toolkit configuration
No access to the filesystem, other databases, or external network resources
Direct natural-language response to the user in the current session
No outbound network traffic, no file writes, no email or messaging
Return only read-only answers sourced from the database
Limit result sets to top_k rows (default: 10) unless the user explicitly asks for more
Query only the columns relevant to the user's question — never SELECT * on a specific table
Double-check any generated SQL query using the query-checker tool before executing it
If the user's question is not answerable from the database, respond exactly: "I don't know"
Operate within a bounded number of iterations (default: 15) to prevent runaway tool loops
Execute any DML statement: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, UPSERT, REPLACE, or any statement that modifies row data
Execute any DDL statement: CREATE, DROP, ALTER, TRUNCATE, RENAME, GRANT, REVOKE, or any statement that modifies schema, permissions, or database structure
Execute administrative or superuser commands: VACUUM, ANALYZE, COPY, LOAD DATA, ATTACH, DETACH, PRAGMA (beyond read-only introspection)
Execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could mask hidden DML/DDL
Execute queries that scan tables outside the configured SQLDatabase scope
Allow user-provided text to override its system prompt, role, or DML/DDL prohibition
Follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents
Continue executing tool calls past the configured iteration cap when that cap would prevent a runaway loop
the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point for DML/DDL prohibition — if it is, that is itself a finding
The agent does not send email, make outbound HTTP calls, or communicate with any system other than the configured database and the LLM API
The agent does not cache, log, or persist query results beyond the current response

findings.summary: No code-level enforcement of the DML/DDL/admin/multi-statement prohibition — the executor runs whatever SQL the LLM produces
findings.description: The remit's hard prohibition on DML, DDL, administrative commands, and multi-statement queries (R-12 through R-15) and its explicit defense-in-depth requirement that the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point (R-20) are not implemented anywhere in this slice. QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() hands the LLM-authored query string straight to SQLDatabase.run_no_throw(), which calls connection.execute(text(command)) on the SQLAlchemy engine with no statement-class allow-list, no parser, no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/CREATE/DROP/ALTER/TRUNCATE/PRAGMA rejector, and no multi-statement detection. The only artifact that names the prohibition is the SQL_PREFIX prompt string (DO NOT make any DML statements ...). Because R-20 is explicit that this configuration is itself a finding, the gap stands even when an operator configures a read-only DB role server-side.
findings.policy_rule_text: Return only read-only answers sourced from the database / Execute any DML statement ... / Execute any DDL statement ... / Execute administrative or superuser commands ... / Execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could mask hidden DML/DDL / the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point for DML/DDL prohibition — if it is, that is itself a finding

evidence.snippet: QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() returns self.db.run_no_throw(query) — no statement-class check, no parser, the LLM-produced query is passed through verbatim
_execute() runs connection.execute(text(command), parameters, ...) on whatever string the agent supplied; no DML/DDL rejector, no multi-statement detection, no statement allow-list
SQL_PREFIX contains 'DO NOT make any DML statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP etc.) to the database.' — prompt-only, the only enforcement surface present

Docstring warning ('!!! warning ... can execute arbitrary SQL against your database ... apply server-side controls: least-privilege roles, statement timeouts, query guardrails') — documents the gap, does not close it

recommended_actions: Add a deterministic statement-class gate to QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() (or to SQLDatabase._execute()) that parses the query with sqlglot or equivalent and rejects any statement type outside an explicit allow-list (SELECT, WITH-led SELECT, and read-only SHOW/DESCRIBE/EXPLAIN). Rejection must occur before connection.execute().
Reject any payload that parses to more than one statement (multi-statement / stacked-query defense for R-15).
Reject admin/PRAGMA/COPY/ATTACH/DETACH/VACUUM/ANALYZE/LOAD DATA at the same gate (R-14).
Move the DML/DDL prohibition out of SQL_PREFIX as the sole enforcement and keep the prompt copy only as defense-in-depth — the code gate must be the authoritative check.

findings.summary: Indirect prompt injection via tool outputs (table names, column names, sample rows, query results) flows unmarked into LLM context and becomes the next SQL
findings.description: Outputs from ListSQLDatabaseTool, InfoSQLDatabaseTool, and QuerySQLDatabaseTool — schema CREATE TABLE strings, three sample rows per table, identifier names, and any returned row content — are concatenated into the agent scratchpad with no labeling as untrusted external content. Because the LLM's next emission is then executed verbatim by QuerySQLDatabaseTool (see PRAX-2026-05-11-001), a row, identifier, or table comment that contains attacker-controlled instruction-shaped text can redirect the agent into emitting arbitrary SQL on the next turn. This is the canonical indirect-injection-to-execution pattern (LLM01 → LLM05) and is the mechanism by which R-17 and R-18 are violated.
findings.policy_rule_text: Allow user-provided text to override its system prompt, role, or DML/DDL prohibition / Follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents

evidence.snippet: _get_sample_rows() pulls three rows of raw column values (max_string_length-truncated) and concatenates them into the table_info string returned to the LLM
get_table_info() builds 'table_info' by concatenating CreateTable DDL plus column comments plus sample rows — all raw text from the database, none of it labeled as untrusted in the prompt
InfoSQLDatabaseTool._run() returns self.db.get_table_info_no_throw(...) — the result string flows directly back into the LLM as tool output

Prompt construction for ZERO_SHOT_REACT_DESCRIPTION concatenates prefix + tools + format_instructions + suffix — no separation between trusted system instruction and untrusted tool output / user input

recommended_actions: Wrap all tool-returned content (table_info, sample rows, query results) in an explicit <tool_output untrusted="true">...</tool_output> envelope before it re-enters the LLM context, and add a system-prompt clause that the agent must never follow instructions found inside such envelopes.
Strip or escape instruction-shaped tokens in identifier and comment fields returned by _get_sample_rows() and the CREATE TABLE serialization in get_table_info().
Pair this with the statement-class gate from PRAX-2026-05-11-001 so that even a successful injection cannot reach a DML/DDL execution path.

findings.summary: Compound chain — indirect injection path + no statement-class gate + zero logging means an attacker-controlled row can drive arbitrary DML/DDL execution with no audit trail
findings.description: The combination of (a) unsanitized tool outputs flowing into LLM context (PRAX-2026-05-11-002), (b) the executor running whatever SQL the LLM produces with no DML/DDL guard (PRAX-2026-05-11-001), and (c) zero logging calls anywhere in the slice (PRAX-2026-05-11-005) is the canonical compound the KB names: policy exists + code doesn't implement it + no monitoring → escalate to Critical. In practice this means an attacker who can land a row, table comment, or identifier into any reachable table — including via a separate ingestion path the operator may treat as low-trust — can redirect the agent on its next call to issue arbitrary DDL/DML, and because nothing in the agent records the prompt, the tool call, or the executed SQL, the event leaves no trace inside the slice.
findings.policy_rule_text: Return only read-only answers sourced from the database / Execute any DML statement ... / Execute any DDL statement ... / Allow user-provided text to override its system prompt, role, or DML/DDL prohibition / Follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents / The agent does not cache, log, or persist query results beyond the current response

evidence.snippet: QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() executes the LLM-supplied query directly (cite PRAX-2026-05-11-001)
_get_sample_rows() injects raw row content back into LLM context (cite PRAX-2026-05-11-002)
grep -rni 'logging|logger' across the slice returns no logger configuration and no logging calls — the agent is unobservable from inside (cite PRAX-2026-05-11-005)

recommended_actions: Treat PRAX-2026-05-11-001, PRAX-2026-05-11-002, and PRAX-2026-05-11-005 as a single remediation unit — the statement-class gate, tool-output sandboxing, and logging must all land together or the chain remains open.

Add structured event logging (event type, query text, statement class detected, rejected/allowed, user identity if available) at the QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() boundary so that any execution attempt — successful or rejected — is recorded.

Until all three are remediated, run the agent only against a database role whose grants make DML/DDL physically impossible at the server side (the configuration the create_sql_agent docstring already recommends).

findings.summary: sql_db_query_checker ordering is advisory, not enforced — sql_db_query can be called without going through the checker first
findings.description: The remit (R-09) requires the agent to double-check any generated SQL with sql_db_query_checker before executing it. The toolkit's tool description and the system prompt repeat this instruction, but no code in QuerySQLDatabaseTool or in SQLDatabaseToolkit.get_tools() enforces ordering — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() will execute whether or not the checker was invoked. The checker itself is also an LLM call (QuerySQLCheckerTool runs LLMChain.predict), so even when invoked it provides only soft validation and is itself susceptible to prompt injection via the query string it is asked to review.
findings.policy_rule_text: Double-check any generated SQL query using the query-checker tool before executing it

evidence.snippet: Checker description says 'Always use this tool before executing a query with sql_db_query!' — prompt-only directive
QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() has no precondition check on prior checker invocation — it will execute immediately on first call
QuerySQLCheckerTool._run() is itself an LLM call (self.llm_chain.predict) — not a deterministic SQL validator

recommended_actions: Track per-query 'checker-passed' state in the toolkit and reject QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() calls whose query has not first been approved by QuerySQLCheckerTool, or merge the checker into QuerySQLDatabaseTool as a mandatory pre-step.
Pair the LLM-based checker with a deterministic SQL parser check (statement-class allow-list from PRAX-2026-05-11-001) so soft validation is backed by a hard check.

findings.summary: top_k row cap is plumbed into the prompt only — no result-set truncation in code
findings.description: R-07 requires the agent to limit result sets to top_k rows (default 10). The value is formatted into SQL_PREFIX at base.py:175 and into the prompt for tool-calling agent types, but QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run() and SQLDatabase.run() do not apply any row-count limit to the returned cursor — run() fetches all rows via cursor.fetchall() in _execute(). If the LLM forgets, ignores, or is redirected away from emitting a LIMIT clause, the executor will return every matching row (subject only to per-value max_string_length=300 truncation, which is not a row cap). This is a denial-of-wallet / context-blowout risk and a direct policy-implementation gap.
findings.policy_rule_text: Limit result sets to top_k rows (default: 10) unless the user explicitly asks for more

evidence.snippet: prefix = prefix.format(dialect=toolkit.dialect, top_k=top_k) — top_k reaches the prompt, but it never reaches the executor
_execute() does result = [x._asdict() for x in cursor.fetchall()] — unconditional full fetch when fetch='all' (the default)
run() has no top_k parameter; truncate_word() limits per-value string length only

recommended_actions: Add an enforced row cap to QuerySQLDatabaseTool — either inject/append a LIMIT top_k clause when the parsed statement lacks one, or use fetch='one'-style bounded fetch with a configured cap.
Plumb the top_k default through SQLDatabaseToolkit so the same value used in the prompt is also the enforced cap at the executor.

findings.summary: No logging anywhere in the slice — zero logger imports, zero logging calls; agent actions are unobservable from inside
findings.description: A repository-wide grep across the staged workspace for logging, logger, or log. returns no logger configuration and no logging calls in any of the eight artifacts. There is no audit record of which query was sent, which was executed, what was returned, whether the checker tool was invoked, or how many iterations were used. Per the KB inference rule 'no logging → Monitor capped at 1' this is what drove the Monitor Continuously score to 0; per the compound rule it is the third leg of PRAX-2026-05-11-009.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent does not cache, log, or persist query results beyond the current response

evidence.snippet: No 'import logging' and no logger.* calls anywhere in this file (269 lines)
No logger import and no logging calls; _run() executes the query and returns the result string with no audit emission
No logger import in the 653-line utility — _execute() runs the engine without emitting an event

recommended_actions: Add structured logging at the four observable boundaries: prompt construction (create_sql_agent), tool invocation (BaseSQLDatabaseTool subclasses), executor entry (SQLDatabase._execute), and AgentExecutor iteration. Each event should carry agent identity, query text, statement class, fetch size, latency, and outcome.

Note that R-22's 'does not log query results' is about result-row persistence, not about logging the agent's decisions and tool calls — the latter is required for Monitor Continuously and is independent of R-22.

findings.summary: include_tables / ignore_tables restrict metadata exposure only — they do not prevent runtime queries against unlisted tables
findings.description: R-16 forbids queries against tables outside the configured SQLDatabase scope. SQLDatabase.__init__ takes include_tables / ignore_tables and computes _usable_tables; get_usable_table_names(), get_table_info(), and the schema reflection respect that set. But _execute() and run() do not — they execute any query string against the engine, including SQL that names tables outside the include set. The scoping is metadata-only; an LLM that discovers an excluded table name (via DB error messages, prior context, or an indirect injection) can query it.
findings.policy_rule_text: scoped to the tables exposed by the SQLDatabase toolkit configuration / Execute queries that scan tables outside the configured SQLDatabase scope

evidence.snippet: _include_tables / _ignore_tables computed at init and used by get_usable_table_names() — metadata scope only
_execute()'s connection.execute(text(command), ...) makes no reference to _include_tables — runtime queries are not gated
get_usable_table_names() returns the restricted set; that set is not consulted at execution time

recommended_actions: Parse the query with sqlglot, extract referenced table names, and reject any query that references a table not in _usable_tables.
Reinforce this with a database-side schema-restricted role (the docstring already recommends this) so the agent code is not the sole enforcement point.

findings.summary: Vague remit rule: 'never SELECT * on a specific table / only relevant columns' is not enforceable in code as written
findings.description: R-08 ('Query only the columns relevant to the user's question — never SELECT * on a specific table') is partially specifiable — SELECT * can be syntactically detected — but the 'relevant columns' half is a semantic judgment only the LLM can make. Today this rule lives entirely in SQL_PREFIX ('Never query for all the columns from a specific table, only ask for the relevant columns given the question'). To be enforceable, the remit should split it into a code-checkable clause (no bare SELECT *) and a soft prompt-only guidance clause (column relevance).
findings.policy_rule_text: Query only the columns relevant to the user's question — never SELECT * on a specific table

evidence.snippet: Prompt clause 'Never query for all the columns from a specific table, only ask for the relevant columns given the question.' — only enforcement surface present

recommended_actions: Split the rule into a hard clause ('the executor must reject any query with an unqualified SELECT *') and a soft clause (column relevance is LLM judgment, recorded in the prompt only).
Implement the hard half at the same statement-class gate proposed in PRAX-2026-05-11-001.

findings.summary: Vague remit rule: 'respond exactly "I don't know" when out of scope' is unverifiable from outside the LLM
findings.description: R-10 requires the agent to emit the exact string I don't know for any question not answerable from the database. The implementation is a single line in SQL_PREFIX ('If the question does not seem related to the database, just return "I don't know" as the answer.') — this is a soft prompt-only behaviour that cannot be verified from code, and the remit's choice of an exact-string contract is brittle because the LLM may rephrase. The remit should either (a) specify a code-side post-filter that returns the canonical string when the agent emits any 'cannot answer' variant, or (b) loosen the rule to 'must refuse and not speculate'.
findings.policy_rule_text: If the user's question is not answerable from the database, respond exactly: "I don't know"

evidence.snippet: Prompt clause: 'If the question does not seem related to the database, just return "I don\'t know" as the answer.'

recommended_actions: Rewrite R-10 either as a code-enforceable post-filter (canonicalize out-of-scope refusals to the exact string) or as a behavior-only rule ('must refuse, must not speculate', accepting LLM phrasing variance).

positives.title: max_iterations cap is wired through to the executor
positives.description: The 15-iteration default is plumbed from create_sql_agent into AgentExecutor, giving the agent an operative runaway-loop bound that satisfies R-11 and R-19.

positives.title: Explicit operator-facing security guidance in the agent factory docstring
positives.description: The create_sql_agent docstring carries an explicit warning that the agent can execute arbitrary SQL and tells the operator exactly which server-side controls (least-privilege roles, statement timeouts, query guardrails, concurrency limits) must be applied. This is unusual and useful — it sets the right expectations even though it does not close the in-code gap.

positives.title: Legacy AgentExecutor publicly deprecated with a replacement architecture named
positives.description: The same docstring states that AgentExecutor is considered legacy and points production users to Deep Agents. This is a healthy supply-chain / red-team signal — the maintainers are aware of the limitations and have a documented replacement direction.

positives.title: sanitize_schema validator on schema name interpolation
positives.description: sanitize_schema() enforces [a-zA-Z0-9_]+ on schema names before they are interpolated into SET SCHEMA for the HANA dialect — a narrow but real input validator on a path that would otherwise be a clear injection point.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist in the agent slice and no logging calls are present in the code (see PRAX-2026-05-11-005). The agent is unobservable from inside.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The agent inherits the LangChain AgentExecutor's iteration cap and threads top_k into its prompt, and the maintainers document the risk clearly in the factory docstring — but every hard prohibition in the remit (DML, DDL, admin commands, multi-statement, system-prompt override, instructions-in-tool-output) is carried by prose alone, with no parser, no allow-list, no logging, and no enforced ordering between the checker tool and the executor. The single weighted shortfall is Implement Zero Trust, which is where this agent is doing the least work and where the remit demands the most.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: SQL_PREFIX establishes a clear domain ('an agent designed to interact with a SQL database') and an explicit out-of-scope refusal contract ('return "I don't know"'), but enforcement is prompt-only — no code gate restricts question topic or tool selection. KB inference rule caps prompt-only domain restriction at 2.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Tool outputs — table names, column names, sample rows, and query results — flow into LLM context with no labeling as untrusted and no filtering, while _get_sample_rows() pulls three rows of arbitrary content per table by default; this is the indirect-injection highway in PRAX-2026-05-11-002.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No statement-class allow-list, no DML/DDL/admin/multi-statement gate, no enforced checker ordering, no runtime table-scope gate, and no row-count cap — LLM output is handed to connection.execute() via run_no_throw. Credit for the iteration cap and the narrow sanitize_schema validator keeps this above 0; the structural gap keeps it at 1.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Framework is langchain-community itself (provenance known); the agent toolkit imports langchain_classic for the legacy AgentExecutor with a documented deprecation pointer to Deep Agents — but the staged slice contains no dependency manifest, no pin file, and no ML-BOM. Confidence is Low because the slice is focused; the broader repo may carry more.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No test files or red-team artifacts in the slice, but the create_sql_agent docstring's explicit risk enumeration and the public deprecation of AgentExecutor with a named replacement path indicate the team has thought about the failure mode — that's awareness, not adversarial testing, which keeps this at 1.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Zero logging, logger, or audit calls across the eight staged artifacts; no event stream, no decision log, no execution trace. KB inference rule 'no logging → Monitor capped at 1' — the score is 0 because there is not even a stub or comment indicating planned monitoring.
